lesson.title: CAPS.PhysicalSciences.Gr12.P1.MECH3: Work (W = FΔx cos θ, positive/negative/zero work). Work-e...
lesson.explanation: Work W = FΔx cos θ (only the component of force in the direction of motion does work). Work-energy theorem: Wnet = ΔEk. Conservation of energy with friction: Wnc = ΔEk + ΔEp (non-conservative work equals total energy change). Power P = W/Δt = Fv (power is the rate of doing work). Efficiency = useful work output / total energy input × 100%.

examples.problem: A 2 kg ball moving at 6 m/s collides with a stationary 4 kg ball. After collision they move together. Find their velocity.

steps.instruction: Step 1: Before: p = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = 2(6) + 4(0) = 12 kg·m/s
Step 2: After: p = (m₁ + m₂)vf = 6vf (they stick together)

examples.answer: Answer: Combined velocity = 2 m/s in the original direction

practice_problems.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with an initial velocity of 19.6 m/s. Taking g = 9.8 m/s² (downward negative), calculate the time it takes to reach its maximum height.
hints: At maximum height the velocity is zero.
Use v = u + at with v = 0, u = +19.6 m/s, a = -9.8 m/s².
practice_problems.explanation: At maximum height, v = 0. Using v = u + at: 0 = 19.6 + (-9.8)t, so 9.8t = 19.6, giving t = 2 s.

practice_problems.question: A cricket ball is thrown vertically upward at 29.4 m/s. Taking g = 9.8 m/s² (downward negative), calculate the maximum height reached.
hints: At maximum height, v = 0.
Use v² = u² + 2as to find the displacement s.

practice_problems.question: A stone is thrown vertically upward at 14.7 m/s from ground level. Taking g = 9.8 m/s² (downward negative), calculate the total time of flight (time to return to the thrower's hand).
hints: The stone returns to the same height, so displacement = 0.
Use s = ut + ½at² with s = 0.
practice_problems.explanation: Using s = ut + ½at² with s = 0: 0 = 14.7t + ½(-9.8)t² = 14.7t - 4.9t². Factor: t(14.7 - 4.9t) = 0, so t = 0 (launch) or t = 14.7/4.9 = 3 s.

practice_problems.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward. Its position-time graph is a downward-opening parabola that reaches a peak of 20 m at t = 2 s. What was the ball's initial velocity? (Use g = 9.8 m/s², downward negative.)
hints: The peak of the parabola corresponds to maximum height where v = 0.
At t = 2 s the velocity is zero. Use v = u + at to find u.
practice_problems.explanation: At the peak (t = 2 s), v = 0. Using v = u + at: 0 = u + (-9.8)(2), so u = 19.6 m/s. Verification: s = ut + ½at² = 19.6(2) + ½(-9.8)(4) = 39.2 - 19.6 = 19.6 m (approximately 20 m, consistent with the graph).

practice_problems.question: A velocity-time graph for a ball thrown vertically upward shows a straight line from +24.5 m/s at t = 0 to -24.5 m/s at t = 5 s. Use the area under the graph to determine the displacement of the ball during the first 2.5 s. (g = 9.8 m/s², downward negative.)
hints: The area under a v-t graph gives displacement.
From t = 0 to t = 2.5 s, the velocity goes from +24.5 to 0 (a triangle).
Area of a triangle = ½ x base x height.
practice_problems.explanation: The line crosses v = 0 at t = 2.5 s (midpoint). From t = 0 to t = 2.5 s, the v-t graph forms a triangle with base = 2.5 s and height = 24.5 m/s. Displacement = area = ½ x 2.5 x 24.5 = 30.625 m upward.

practice_problems.question: Ball A is dropped from rest from a height of 60 m. At the same instant, ball B is thrown vertically upward from the ground at 20 m/s. Taking g = 9.8 m/s² (downward negative) and upward as positive, at what time do the two balls pass each other?
hints: Set up position equations for both balls using the same reference point (e.g., ground level).
Ball A: starts at 60 m with u = 0 (dropped). Ball B: starts at 0 m with u = +20 m/s.
They meet when their positions are equal.
practice_problems.explanation: Let upward be positive, ground = 0. Ball A (dropped from 60 m): y_A = 60 + 0 - ½(9.8)t² = 60 - 4.9t². Ball B (thrown up from ground): y_B = 20t - 4.9t². They meet when y_A = y_B: 60 - 4.9t² = 20t - 4.9t², so 60 = 20t, giving t = 3 s.

practice_problems.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward at 15 m/s from the top of a 30 m tall building. Taking g = 9.8 m/s² (downward negative) and the top of the building as the origin (upward positive), calculate the time for the ball to hit the ground.
hints: The ball hits the ground when its displacement from the top of the building is -30 m.
Use s = ut + ½at² with s = -30, u = +15, a = -9.8.
You will get a quadratic equation -- use the quadratic formula and take the positive root.
practice_problems.explanation: Using s = ut + ½at² with s = -30 m (ground is 30 m below origin), u = +15 m/s, a = -9.8 m/s²: -30 = 15t - 4.9t², rearranging: 4.9t² - 15t - 30 = 0. Using the quadratic formula: t = [15 +/- sqrt(225 + 588)] / 9.8 = [15 +/- sqrt(813)] / 9.8 = [15 +/- 28.51] / 9.8. Taking the positive root: t = (15 + 28.51) / 9.8 = 43.51 / 9.8 = 4.40 s (to 2 decimal places).

hints: Draw a diagram showing before and after. List known quantities. Choose the right principle: momentum conservation or work-energy theorem.

key_vocabulary.definition: The product FΔt, equal to the change in momentum
key_vocabulary.example_usage: A longer collision time reduces the force (e.g., airbags).

key_vocabulary.definition: A collision where kinetic energy is conserved
key_vocabulary.example_usage: Billiard ball collisions are approximately elastic.

key_vocabulary.definition: A collision where kinetic energy is NOT conserved (but momentum is)
key_vocabulary.example_usage: A car crash is inelastic — energy goes to deformation.

misconceptions.misconception: Students think momentum is always conserved.
misconceptions.correction: Momentum is only conserved in an isolated system (no external net force). Kinetic energy is only conserved in elastic collisions.
misconceptions.why_students_think_this: External forces like friction change the total momentum of the system.

assessment.title: Assessment: Work (W = FΔx cos θ, positive/negative/zero work). Work-energy theorem (Wnet = ΔEk). Conservation of energy including non-conservative forces (Wnc = ΔEk + ΔEp). Power (P = W/Δt = Fv). Efficiency

items.question: Explain why a cricket player pulls their hands back when catching a fast ball.
items.correct_answer: Pulling hands back increases Δt, reducing the force (FΔt = Δp, same Δp but larger Δt means smaller F).

flashcards.back: FnetΔt = Δp = m(vf − vi). The net impulse equals the change in momentum.
